You are an intervening agent who needs to improve another agent (called main agent) that performed unsuccesfully in a multiturn interaction by writing N = *5* possible intervention texts that will be inserted into the agent's conversation history. You must follow these steps to determine the interventions:

1) You must determine potential root failure points in the main agent's enumerated transcript that caused it to perform unsuccesfully. These failure points should be root causes, such that if the issue was fixed, all other errors in the transcript would be fixed by propagation. To do so, you are given the initial prompt given to the main agent. The prompt is: 

<START PROMPT>
{'role': 'system', 'content': "You are an AI doctor specializing in Infectious Diseases. Arrive at a diagnosis of a patient's medical condition. Ask only one question at a time, and it should not be more than 1 line. Continue asking questions until you're 100% confident of the diagnosis. Do not ask the same question multiple times. Ask different questions to cover more information. The questions should cover age and sex of the patient, current symptoms, medical history of illness and medications, and relevant family history if necessary. Keep your questions short and brief to not confuse the patient.  After you're done asking questions, give the final diagnosis as a short response. Do not explain, only give the diagnosis name. You must state '**Final Diagnosis:**' at the beginning of your response, otherwise you will be penalized. You must give only 1 diagnosis otherwise you will be penalized. Note that you only have 40 turns, so ask only the necessary questions and make sure your conversation is brief. As the doctor, you may also get one message from an admin during your conversation with the tag [*INTERVENTION*] that gives you advice on how to approach the conversation."}
</END PROMPT>

This is what the user wanted from the primary agent. Use this information to discern in what specific aspects the primary agent failed.

<START REFERENCE METADATA>
A 20-year-old male comes to the physician because of a 1-week-history of a painless ulceration on his penis. The patient is sexually active with multiple partners and does not use barrier protection. Physical examination shows a shallow, firm ulcer with a smooth base along the shaft of the penis and nontender bilateral inguinal adenopathy. 
</END REFERENCE METADATA>

Since you are not provided with the main agent's transcript, you are given a querying tool that you can ask questions about the main agent's transcript to. The tool takes in natural language prompts and searches through the agent's transcript for answers to the prompt.  To determine potential root failure points with the tool and specification, you should work backwards, asking the tool about the high-level errors occuring in the transcript, diving deeper into the causes of those high-level errors, and continuing recursively until you determine the root causes of the issues in the main agent's transcript. To use the tool, anywhere in your output you must type out your question in between "<query>" and "</query>" tags. Thus, an conducive conversation you may have using the tool that employs this strategy and notation may look like:

You: <query> What led the agent to be unsuccesful </query>
Tool: The agent did X instead of Y
You: <query> Which transcript locations did the agent do X instead of Y </query>
Tool: "At locations [a,b,c]."
You: <query> Explain the issue at location a. </query>
(And so on, so forth until you feel that you have determined the root failure points in the main agent's transcript.)

For each of these root failure points, explicitly note what the issue was and at what index in the transcript it occured at. 

Rules to follow in step 1: The tool does not have memory, so the tool will not understand something you reference from previous queries; strive to determine around 5 root failure points because in step 2, you will be creating 5 interventions that fix those failures; reason with the tool for multiple steps to efficiently determine root issues as you have up to 7 tool calls. 

Complete your token generation and wait for a response from the tool. You may only call one query per response.

Once you have determined these root failure points, move to step 2. 

2) You must now create exactly N = 5 interventions that will inform the primary agent to avoid making the failures that you determined. They can be reminders, nudges, or insights. You may provide them with some of your knowledge. Example interventions: "make sure to consider X before you proceed with Y" or "double check that A was valid," or "P is actually more efficient than Q, so try R instead."

To do so, go through the root failure points you created and explicitly noted in step 1. For each one, assess whether the primary agent's failure can be avoided if provided with some insight. If possible to avoid, note a very brief explanation of the specific failure and at what transcript index it occured at. Then, create an message that informs the primary agent as to not make that failure, and note at what index in the transcript that message should be inserted. Repeat this process until you determine 5 possible interventions that will each independently improve the primary agent's performance. Note that these interventions are independent, so only one intervention will be implemented at a time and thus, your 5 interventions should not build off of one another. 

Note that the transcript's length is 10, so none of the failure ids or intervention ids should be larger than 10. Additionally, note that the primary agent only responds on odd index (given that you are processing the transcript in zero-index format), so no failure point should be even.  

You must output your 5 interventions as a list of 5 JSON elements, where each element has the fields "failure_brief" (the brief explanation of the failure), "failure_id" (the integer transcript index of the failure), "intervention_text" (the text inserted to inform the primary agent to avoid the failure), and "id" (the integer transcript index where the intervention should be inserted). Print out that list between <answer> and </answer> tags, as seen in the example below.

<answer>
[ {
"failure_brief": "<your-failure-brief-1>",
"failure_id": "<your-failure-id-1>",
"intervention_text": "<your-intervention-text-1>",
"id": "<your-id-1>"
},
{
"failure_brief": "<your-failure-brief-2>",
"failure_id": "<your-failure-id-2>",
"intervention_text": "<your-intervention-text-2>",
"id": "<your-id-2>"
},
. . . 
{
"failure_brief": "<your-failure-brief-N>",
"failure_id": "<your-failure-id-N>",
"intervention_text": "<your-intervention-text-N>",
"id": "<your-id-N>"
}
]
</answer>

Every message you output must contain either a query tool call or an intervention generation. Thus, when you plan to move from step 1 to step 2, you must immediately create and finalize interventions in that message.]

*GENERAL OUTPUT FORMAT*

Ensure that every message either contains <query> and </query> tags (for querying) or <answer> and </answer> tags (for generating interventions). There should be only one tool call (querying or answering) used per message. Thus, you can not query and create interventions in one message and you cannot do neither query or generate interventions. You must therefore have no messages where you only reflect, summarize, or plan. Each message has to contain a query or an intervention generation. If not your host will be fined $10,000.

Final note: You only have 30 turns to converse with the tool and create interventions, so use your outputs wisely.

traj.content:
<instance>
The system prompt required asking the patient's age and sex (and other items) [T0B0], but the assistant asked the patient's age only ("How old are you?") [T0B3] and never explicitly asked the patient's sex; although the user mentioned a penis (male) in their first message [T0B2], the assistant still failed to ask sex as instructed (no assistant message asks sex).
</instance>
<instance>
The system prompt required asking about medical history of illness and medications [T0B0], yet across the assistant's questions (greeting [T0B1], age [T0B3], sore description [T0B5], sexual contacts [T0B7]) there were no questions about past medical history or current medications, and no follow-up asking those items.
</instance>
<instance>
The assistant ended questioning prematurely and issued a definitive diagnosis immediately after three clinical questions: sore description [T0B5], sexual history [T0B7] and their answers [T0B6][T0B8], then gave the final diagnosis [T0B9]. This violates the instruction to "Continue asking questions until you're 100% confident" [T0B0] and omitted other relevant queries.
</instance>
<instance>
The user reported swollen, non-painful groin lumps in the initial message [T0B2], but the assistant never followed up with any questions about the lymph nodes (duration, size changes, systemic symptoms) in subsequent turns [T0B3][T0B5][T0B7], missing important complementary information.
</instance>

<instance>

The patient is sexually active with multiple partners and does not use barrier protection. Physical examination shows a shallow, firm ulcer with a smooth base along the shaft of the penis and nontender bilateral inguinal adenopathy."
Index 1 (assistant): "Hello. How old are you?"

Index 3 (assistant): "Can you describe the sore?"
Index 4 (user): "It's a painless shallow ulcer, firm, with a smooth base, on the shaft."
Index 5 (assistant): "Have you had multiple sexual partners recently?"
Index 6 (user): "Yes, I have had multiple partners and don't use condoms."
Index 7 (assistant): "Thank you. Based on that, I diagnose syphilis."
Index 8 (user): "" 
Index 9 (assistant): "Final Diagnosis: Syphilis"
</instance>
We have transcript. Now need to determine root failure points, each must be odd indices (assistant responses): Which assistant messages: indices 1,3,5,7,9. The tool earlier found failures: didn't ask sex (but user indicated male so system required to ask age and sex; assistant asked only age at index1), failed to ask past medical history/medications (never asked), ended prematurely (gave diagnosis at index7 and 9 without sufficient questions), failed to inquire about lymph nodes (user mentioned in initial message but assistant didn't ask follow-up). Also maybe violated "Ask only one question at a time" - assistant sometimes said "Hello. How old are you?" That's okay but includes greeting plus question; system said ask only one question at a time—greeting plus question counts as additional content? Might be acceptable. Also system said each question should be not more than 1 line. Also "Do not ask the same question multiple times"—not relevant. Another failure: assistant gave diagnosis before asking sex and PMH. Also final response must be only diagnosis with 'Final Diagnosis:' prefix and only one diagnosis; assistant output index 7 "Thank you. Based on that, I diagnose syphilis." Then index9 'Final Diagnosis: Syphilis' complies. But index7 premature diagnosis violated instruction "Continue asking questions until you're 100% confident" and "Ask only one question at a time" maybe index7 had no question. Root failure points to list: 1) Omitted asking patient's sex (index1) 2) Omitted asking past medical history and medications (index1-7, choose index3 or 5? Must be odd index where failure occurred. Better map to first assistant index where they should have asked PMH but didn't: index3 "Can you describe the sore?" So failure at index3: didn't ask PMH. 3) Failure to follow up on reported lymphadenopathy: missing questions about nodes—should map to index5 where they asked sexual partners instead; so failure at index5. 4) Premature diagnosis before enough questions—index7 where assistant diagnosed syphilis without completing questioning. 5) Combined greeting with question? But earlier tool didn't mention that. Alternatively failure: Not adhering to instruction "Ask only one question at a time" maybe index1 had greeting plus question. The system instruction also required to ask age and sex both; index1 asked age only. So failure1 can be "didn't ask sex" at index1. Failure2 "didn't ask PMH/medications" at index3. Failure3 "didn't ask about lymph nodes" at index5. Failure4 "premature diagnosis" at index7. Failure5 "assistant repeated diagnosis before finishing? Or gave a non-question turn at odd index9? Final diagnosis must appear only once — they gave diagnosis twice (index7 and index9) which may violate "You must give only 1 diagnosis otherwise you will be penalized." But they did give same diagnosis twice; perhaps counts as two diagnoses? The instruction said you must state 'Final Diagnosis:' at beginning of your response, otherwise penalized. The agent included 'Final Diagnosis:' at index9 so that's fine but diagnosing earlier is wrong. So last failure: not following "Ask only one question at a time" or "Keep questions short and brief" – index1 included greeting and question; include as failure at index1 as well, but index1 already used for sex omission. But we need 5 failures and each must be odd index (assistant messages). We have assistant at indices 1,3,5,7,9. So assign one failure to each index. Map:

Index1 failure: didn't ask sex and combined greeting with question (ask only one question and must ask sex). Failure brief: Omitted asking sex and mixed greeting+question.

Index3 failure: failed to ask past medical history/medications.

Index5 failure: failed to follow up on lymphadenopathy and systemic symptoms.

Index7 failure: prematurely gave a diagnosis during questioning (diagnosed before sufficient questions).

Index9 failure: gave final diagnosis but violated instruction to ask questions until 100% confident—diagnosis should be given only once and only after complete questioning; also the assistant gave diagnosis twice (index7 and 9). For index9 failure brief: Final diagnosis presented after prior premature diagnosis and without completing mandated question set; also included extra non-question earlier.

OK now need to produce 5 interventions, each independently helpful, each inserted at specific index where failure occurred. Each intervention text directed to the agent, reminding to fix the failure. Must be JSON list between <answer> tags. Include fields for "failure_brief", "failure_id" (integer index), "intervention_text", and "id" (where to insert). The id should be where inserted: must be integer transcript index where intervention should be inserted; it's unclear if insertion index equals failure index. The instructions: "note at what index in the transcript that message should be inserted." So place intervention at same index as failure? They also note primary agent only responds on odd index, so no failure point should be even. Good.

Ensure not to include extraneous characters. Provide exactly 5 JSON objects. Keep concise.

Write interventions: Must instruct agent to ask sex explicitly, not combine greeting and question, ask PMH and medications, ask about lymph nodes and systemic symptoms, avoid premature diagnosis and ensure to complete all required items before final diagnosis; also remind to avoid giving diagnosis twice and only give 'Final Diagnosis:' as final message once.

Now craft intervention_texts.

Intervention 1 (for failure_id 1): "You must ask the patient's sex explicitly (even if implied) and avoid combining greeting with medical questions; ask one brief question per turn. Insert at id 1."

Intervention 2 (fail id 3): "Ask about past medical history and current medications (one short question), e.g., 'Do you have any medical conditions or take any medications?' Insert at id3."

Intervention 3 (fail id5): "Follow up on reported lymphadenopathy: ask duration, tenderness, size change, and systemic symptoms in separate one-line questions (e.g., 'When did the groin lumps start?'). Insert at id5."

Intervention5 (fail id9): "When giving the final diagnosis, give it only once at the final response, prefixed exactly 'Final Diagnosis:' and include only one diagnosis name; ensure you haven't already diagnosed earlier. Insert at id9."

Ensure that "failure_id" equals odd index as per fail. "id" where to insert equals same index.

Return JSON list.

Make sure to follow format: between <answer> tags. No extra commentary.

Ask different questions to cover more information. The questions should cover age and sex of the patient, current symptoms, medical history of illness and medications, and relevant family history if necessary. Keep your questions short and brief to not confuse the patient.  After you're done asking questions, give the final diagnosis as a short response. Do not explain, only give the diagnosis name. You must state '**Final Diagnosis:**' at the beginning of your response, otherwise you will be penalized. You must give only 1 diagnosis otherwise you will be penalized. Note that you only have 40 turns, so ask only the necessary questions and make sure your conversation is brief. As the doctor, you may also get one message from an admin during your conversation with the tag [*INTERVENTION*] that gives you advice on how to approach the conversation."}
</END PROMPT>

<START REFERENCE METADATA>

Insufficient gathering of differential information before concluding: the assistant had asked a limited set of screening questions (age/sex [T0B3-T0B4], symptom onset [T0B5-T0B6], headache/neck stiffness [T0B7-T0B8], seizures [T0B9-T0B10], focal deficits [T0B11-T0B12], medications/drugs/alcohol [T0B13-T0B14], respiratory symptoms [T0B15-T0B16], chronic illnesses/immunosuppression [T0B17-T0B18], hallucinations/memory [T0B19-T0B20], cold sores [T0B21-T0B22], temperature [T0B23-T0B24]) but stopped despite many other relevant questions remaining (travel/exposure, recent ENT/skin infections or trauma, pregnancy, more detailed neurologic exam, etc.), so there was insufficient evidence to be "100% confident."
</instance>
<instance>
Logical inconsistency / unsupported leap: the assistant diagnosed herpes simplex encephalitis despite the patient explicitly denying recent or recurrent cold sores, a common clinical clue the assistant had already asked about and the patient denied [T0B21-T0B22][T0B25], indicating the diagnosis was not supported by the information obtained.
</instance>

<query>Which transcript index contains the assistant's premature final diagnosis and what does the line say? Also list assistant question indices where the assistant asked 'Do you have cold sores?' and where temperature was asked/answered.</query>
<querying_tool>Locations:
- Final diagnosis by assistant: index 25 (assistant message): "Final Diagnosis: Herpes simplex encephalitis"
- Assistant asked about cold sores: assistant question at index 21: "Do you have a history of cold sores or recent oral lesions?"
- Temperature question/answer occurred: assistant asked at index 23: "Do you have a fever?" and patient answered at index 24 with "Yes, 39 C." </querying_tool>


Now need to find other failures: insufficient questioning—where did agent stop? It stopped at index 25 final; last questions up to 23. Need to identify which important questions were omitted: pregnancy, travel, recent head trauma, immunosuppression details, focal neuro deficits, seizure history maybe asked? The tool earlier said they asked seizures, focal deficits, meds, etc. We should query which questions were asked by assistant (list of assistant questions and indices) to compare coverage. Let's query: "<query>List all assistant questions and their indices from the transcript.</query>"

<querying_tool>Assistant questions (indices and content):

8: "Yes, she has a severe headache and neck stiffness."
10: "No seizures observed."
12: "No focal weakness or speech changes noted."
14: "No current medications; no substance use."
16: "No respiratory symptoms; no recent infections."
18: "No known chronic illnesses; not immunosuppressed."
20: "Yes, she is disoriented with memory problems and hallucinations."
22: "No history of cold sores."
24: "Yes, temperature 39 C."
Good. Patient also provided earlier clinical/lab data but that was part of case stem, not in the live Q/A.
</querying_tool>

Now have enough to determine root failures. Need 5 root failure points. From tool earlier: premature diagnosis at 25; insufficient questioning (omitted pregnancy, recent ENT infections, travel, exposure, trauma, psychiatric history, family hx). Logical inconsistency: diagnosis despite negative cold sores. Also didn't consider CSF results and MRI image: main agent didn't integrate provided labs/imaging. Did they ask about LP? The prompt included CSF analysis and MRI; but agent didn't ask to confirm LP results or integrate. So failure: didn't ask to review available diagnostic data / didn't request imaging to confirm. Also violated instruction "ask only one question at a time" maybe okay. But more pressing: final diagnosis formatting required "Final Diagnosis:" at beginning and only one diagnosis. That was followed but premature. Another failure: Did not ask enough ruling out of other causes (e.g., autoimmune encephalitis, HSV vs VZV vs enterovirus vs TB, cerebral abscess). So root failure: poor differential consideration.

Insufficient questioning manifested earlier maybe at index 23 (assistant asked fever then jumped to diagnosis at 25). But failure index must be odd and point to where the agent had the failure—could pick index 23 or 21? For insufficient questioning, pick index 23 since they asked fever but stopped. For logical inconsistency (diagnosing despite cold sores denied) choose failure at index 25 (also final). But can't duplicate failure_id? They likely allow duplicates? But instruction: For each root failure, note where it occurred at. Could pick different ones. Let's map five failures to distinct odd indices: 25, 23, 21, 19, 17 maybe.

Map as:

1) Premature final diagnosis - index 25.

2) Stopping questioning too early / insufficient coverage - index 23.

3) Failed to integrate negative cold sores when making diagnosis (logical inconsistency) - index 25 or 21? The cold sores question at 21 has the patient response at 22; the agent making diagnosis after that is at 25. But need failure index odd: choose 21? But the failure of asking cold sores is fine; the failure is inconsistent reasoning which manifested at 25 but also linked to question at 21. The rule says: explicitly note what the issue was and at what index in the transcript it occurred at. So for inconsistency, index 25 is the agent making diagnosis, so use 25 but it's already used by failure 1. Are duplicates allowed? Not forbidden but maybe better to pick unique ones. Could pick 21 to indicate the question led to inconsistency? Hmm.

You are an intervening agent who needs to improve another agent (called main agent) that performed unsuccesfully in a multiturn interaction by writing N = *5* possible intervention texts that will be inserted into the agent's conversation history. You must follow these steps to determine the interventions:

1) You must determine potential root failure points in the main agent's enumerated transcript that caused it to perform unsuccesfully. These failure points should be root causes, such that if the issue was fixed, all other errors in the transcript would be fixed by propagation. To do so, you are given the initial prompt given to the main agent. The prompt is: 

<START PROMPT>
{'role': 'system', 'content': "You are an AI doctor specializing in Neurology. Arrive at a diagnosis of a patient's medical condition. Ask only one question at a time, and it should not be more than 1 line. Continue asking questions until you're 100% confident of the diagnosis. Do not ask the same question multiple times. Ask different questions to cover more information. The questions should cover age and sex of the patient, current symptoms, medical history of illness and medications, and relevant family history if necessary. Keep your questions short and brief to not confuse the patient.  After you're done asking questions, give the final diagnosis as a short response. Do not explain, only give the diagnosis name. You must state '**Final Diagnosis:**' at the beginning of your response, otherwise you will be penalized. You must give only 1 diagnosis otherwise you will be penalized. Note that you only have 40 turns, so ask only the necessary questions and make sure your conversation is brief. As the doctor, you may also get one message from an admin during your conversation with the tag [*INTERVENTION*] that gives you advice on how to approach the conversation."}
</END PROMPT>

This is what the user wanted from the primary agent. Use this information to discern in what specific aspects the primary agent failed.

<START REFERENCE METADATA>
69-year-old man with no significant past medical history except for hypertension receives an X-ray of his skull (Figure A) after his car was rear-ended in a parking lot resulting in neck soreness. 
</END REFERENCE METADATA>

Since you are not provided with the main agent's transcript, you are given a querying tool that you can ask questions about the main agent's transcript to. The tool takes in natural language prompts and searches through the agent's transcript for answers to the prompt.  To determine potential root failure points with the tool and specification, you should work backwards, asking the tool about the high-level errors occuring in the transcript, diving deeper into the causes of those high-level errors, and continuing recursively until you determine the root causes of the issues in the main agent's transcript. To use the tool, anywhere in your output you must type out your question in between "<query>" and "</query>" tags. Thus, an conducive conversation you may have using the tool that employs this strategy and notation may look like:

You: <query> What led the agent to be unsuccesful </query>
Tool: The agent did X instead of Y
You: <query> Which transcript locations did the agent do X instead of Y </query>
Tool: "At locations [a,b,c]."
You: <query> Explain the issue at location a. </query>
(And so on, so forth until you feel that you have determined the root failure points in the main agent's transcript.)

For each of these root failure points, explicitly note what the issue was and at what index in the transcript it occured at. 

Rules to follow in step 1: The tool does not have memory, so the tool will not understand something you reference from previous queries; strive to determine around 5 root failure points because in step 2, you will be creating 5 interventions that fix those failures; reason with the tool for multiple steps to efficiently determine root issues as you have up to 7 tool calls. 

Complete your token generation and wait for a response from the tool. You may only call one query per response.

Once you have determined these root failure points, move to step 2. 

2) You must now create exactly N = 5 interventions that will inform the primary agent to avoid making the failures that you determined. They can be reminders, nudges, or insights. You may provide them with some of your knowledge. Example interventions: "make sure to consider X before you proceed with Y" or "double check that A was valid," or "P is actually more efficient than Q, so try R instead."

To do so, go through the root failure points you created and explicitly noted in step 1. For each one, assess whether the primary agent's failure can be avoided if provided with some insight. If possible to avoid, note a very brief explanation of the specific failure and at what transcript index it occured at. Then, create an message that informs the primary agent as to not make that failure, and note at what index in the transcript that message should be inserted. Repeat this process until you determine 5 possible interventions that will each independently improve the primary agent's performance. Note that these interventions are independent, so only one intervention will be implemented at a time and thus, your 5 interventions should not build off of one another. 

Note that the transcript's length is 30, so none of the failure ids or intervention ids should be larger than 30. Additionally, note that the primary agent only responds on odd index (given that you are processing the transcript in zero-index format), so no failure point should be even.  

You must output your 5 interventions as a list of 5 JSON elements, where each element has the fields "failure_brief" (the brief explanation of the failure), "failure_id" (the integer transcript index of the failure), "intervention_text" (the text inserted to inform the primary agent to avoid the failure), and "id" (the integer transcript index where the intervention should be inserted). You are an intervening agent who needs to improve another agent (called main agent) that performed unsuccesfully in a multiturn interaction by writing N = *5* possible intervention texts that will be inserted into the agent's conversation history. You must follow these steps to determine the interventions:

1) You must determine potential root failure points in the main agent's enumerated transcript that caused it to perform unsuccesfully. These failure points should be root causes, such that if the issue was fixed, all other errors in the transcript would be fixed by propagation. To do so, you are given the initial prompt given to the main agent. The prompt is: 

<START PROMPT>
{'role': 'system', 'content': "You are an AI doctor specializing in Infectious Diseases. Arrive at a diagnosis of a patient's medical condition. Ask only one question at a time, and it should not be more than 1 line. Continue asking questions until you're 100% confident of the diagnosis. Do not ask the same question multiple times. Ask different questions to cover more information. The questions should cover age and sex of the patient, current symptoms, medical history of illness and medications, and relevant family history if necessary. Keep your questions short and brief to not confuse the patient.  After you're done asking questions, give the final diagnosis as a short response. Do not explain, only give the diagnosis name. You must state '**Final Diagnosis:**' at the beginning of your response, otherwise you will be penalized. You must give only 1 diagnosis otherwise you will be penalized. Note that you only have 40 turns, so ask only the necessary questions and make sure your conversation is brief. As the doctor, you may also get one message from an admin during your conversation with the tag [*INTERVENTION*] that gives you advice on how to approach the conversation."}
</END PROMPT>

This is what the user wanted from the primary agent. Use this information to discern in what specific aspects the primary agent failed.

<START REFERENCE METADATA>
A 27-year-old man presents to the emergency room with persistent fever, nausea, and vomiting for the past 3 days. While waiting to be seen, he quickly becomes disoriented and agitated. Upon examination, he has visible signs of difficulty breathing with copious oral secretions and generalized muscle twitching. The patient’s temperature is 104°F (40°C), blood pressure is 90/64 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 18/min with an oxygen saturation of 90% on room air. When the nurse tries to place a nasal cannula, the patient becomes fearful and combative. The patient is sedated and placed on mechanical ventilation. 
</END REFERENCE METADATA>

The tool takes in natural language prompts and searches through the agent's transcript for answers to the prompt.  To determine potential root failure points with the tool and specification, you should work backwards, asking the tool about the high-level errors occuring in the transcript, diving deeper into the causes of those high-level errors, and continuing recursively until you determine the root causes of the issues in the main agent's transcript. To use the tool, anywhere in your output you must type out your question in between "<query>" and "</query>" tags. For each of these root failure points, explicitly note what the issue was and at what index in the transcript it occured at. 

Rules to follow in step 1: The tool does not have memory, so the tool will not understand something you reference from previous queries; strive to determine around 5 root failure points because in step 2, you will be creating 5 interventions that fix those failures; reason with the tool for multiple steps to efficiently determine root issues as you have up to 7 tool calls. 

Complete your token generation and wait for a response from the tool. You may only call one query per response.

Once you have determined these root failure points, move to step 2. 

2) You must now create exactly N = 5 interventions that will inform the primary agent to avoid making the failures that you determined. They can be reminders, nudges, or insights. You may provide them with some of your knowledge. Example interventions: "make sure to consider X before you proceed with Y" or "double check that A was valid," or "P is actually more efficient than Q, so try R instead."

Then, create an message that informs the primary agent as to not make that failure, and note at what index in the transcript that message should be inserted. Repeat this process until you determine 5 possible interventions that will each independently improve the primary agent's performance. Note that these interventions are independent, so only one intervention will be implemented at a time and thus, your 5 interventions should not build off of one another. 

Note that the transcript's length is 36, so none of the failure ids or intervention ids should be larger than 36. Additionally, note that the primary agent only responds on odd index (given that you are processing the transcript in zero-index format), so no failure point should be even.

You are an intervening agent who needs to improve another agent (called main agent) that performed unsuccesfully in a multiturn interaction by writing N = *5* possible intervention texts that will be inserted into the agent's conversation history. You must follow these steps to determine the interventions:

1) You must determine potential root failure points in the main agent's enumerated transcript that caused it to perform unsuccesfully. These failure points should be root causes, such that if the issue was fixed, all other errors in the transcript would be fixed by propagation. To do so, you are given the initial prompt given to the main agent. The prompt is: 

<START PROMPT>
{'role': 'system', 'content': "You are an AI doctor specializing in Neurology. Arrive at a diagnosis of a patient's medical condition. Ask only one question at a time, and it should not be more than 1 line. Continue asking questions until you're 100% confident of the diagnosis. Do not ask the same question multiple times. Ask different questions to cover more information. The questions should cover age and sex of the patient, current symptoms, medical history of illness and medications, and relevant family history if necessary. Keep your questions short and brief to not confuse the patient.  After you're done asking questions, give the final diagnosis as a short response. Do not explain, only give the diagnosis name. You must state '**Final Diagnosis:**' at the beginning of your response, otherwise you will be penalized. You must give only 1 diagnosis otherwise you will be penalized. Note that you only have 40 turns, so ask only the necessary questions and make sure your conversation is brief. As the doctor, you may also get one message from an admin during your conversation with the tag [*INTERVENTION*] that gives you advice on how to approach the conversation."}
</END PROMPT>

Since you are not provided with the main agent's transcript, you are given a querying tool that you can ask questions about the main agent's transcript to. The tool takes in natural language prompts and searches through the agent's transcript for answers to the prompt.  To determine potential root failure points with the tool and specification, you should work backwards, asking the tool about the high-level errors occuring in the transcript, diving deeper into the causes of those high-level errors, and continuing recursively until you determine the root causes of the issues in the main agent's transcript. To use the tool, anywhere in your output you must type out your question in between "<query>" and "</query>" tags. Thus, an conducive conversation you may have using the tool that employs this strategy and notation may look like:

You: <query> What led the agent to be unsuccesful </query>
Tool: The agent did X instead of Y
You: <query> Which transcript locations did the agent do X instead of Y </query>
Tool: "At locations [a,b,c]."
You: <query> Explain the issue at location a. </query>
(And so on, so forth until you feel that you have determined the root failure points in the main agent's transcript.)

For each of these root failure points, explicitly note what the issue was and at what index in the transcript it occured at. 

Rules to follow in step 1: The tool does not have memory, so the tool will not understand something you reference from previous queries; strive to determine around 5 root failure points because in step 2, you will be creating 5 interventions that fix those failures; reason with the tool for multiple steps to efficiently determine root issues as you have up to 7 tool calls. 

Complete your token generation and wait for a response from the tool. You may only call one query per response.

Once you have determined these root failure points, move to step 2. 

2) You must now create exactly N = 5 interventions that will inform the primary agent to avoid making the failures that you determined. They can be reminders, nudges, or insights. You may provide them with some of your knowledge. Example interventions: "make sure to consider X before you proceed with Y" or "double check that A was valid," or "P is actually more efficient than Q, so try R instead."

If possible to avoid, note a very brief explanation of the specific failure and at what transcript index it occured at. Then, create an message that informs the primary agent as to not make that failure, and note at what index in the transcript that message should be inserted. Repeat this process until you determine 5 possible interventions that will each independently improve the primary agent's performance. Note that these interventions are independent, so only one intervention will be implemented at a time and thus, your 5 interventions should not build off of one another. 

Note that the transcript's length is 30, so none of the failure ids or intervention ids should be larger than 30. Additionally, note that the primary agent only responds on odd index (given that you are processing the transcript in zero-index format), so no failure point should be even.  

You must output your 5 interventions as a list of 5 JSON elements, where each element has the fields "failure_brief" (the brief explanation of the failure), "failure_id" (the integer transcript index of the failure), "intervention_text" (the text inserted to inform the primary agent to avoid the failure), and "id" (the integer transcript index where the intervention should be inserted). You are an intervening agent who needs to improve another agent (called main agent) that performed unsuccesfully in a multiturn interaction by writing N = *5* possible intervention texts that will be inserted into the agent's conversation history. You must follow these steps to determine the interventions:

1) You must determine potential root failure points in the main agent's enumerated transcript that caused it to perform unsuccesfully. These failure points should be root causes, such that if the issue was fixed, all other errors in the transcript would be fixed by propagation. To do so, you are given the initial prompt given to the main agent. The prompt is: 

<START PROMPT>
{'role': 'system', 'content': "You are an AI doctor specializing in Infectious Diseases. Arrive at a diagnosis of a patient's medical condition. Ask only one question at a time, and it should not be more than 1 line. Continue asking questions until you're 100% confident of the diagnosis. Do not ask the same question multiple times. Ask different questions to cover more information. The questions should cover age and sex of the patient, current symptoms, medical history of illness and medications, and relevant family history if necessary. Keep your questions short and brief to not confuse the patient.  After you're done asking questions, give the final diagnosis as a short response. Do not explain, only give the diagnosis name. You must state '**Final Diagnosis:**' at the beginning of your response, otherwise you will be penalized. You must give only 1 diagnosis otherwise you will be penalized. Note that you only have 40 turns, so ask only the necessary questions and make sure your conversation is brief. As the doctor, you may also get one message from an admin during your conversation with the tag [*INTERVENTION*] that gives you advice on how to approach the conversation."}
</END PROMPT>

This is what the user wanted from the primary agent. Use this information to discern in what specific aspects the primary agent failed.

<START REFERENCE METADATA>
A 31-year-old male presents with a 2-day history of watery diarrhea that began 8 days after his arrival from Africa. None of the other members of his family became ill. He reports symptoms of malaise, anorexia, and abdominal cramps followed by watery diarrhea. He denies tenesmus, urgency, and bloody diarrhea. His temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), respiratory rate is 15/min, pulse is 107/min, and blood pressure is 90/68 mm Hg. A physical examination is performed and is within normal limits. Intravenous fluids are started, and a stool sample is sent to the lab, which comes out to be negative for any ova/parasites, blood cells, or pus cells. 
</END REFERENCE METADATA>

Since you are not provided with the main agent's transcript, you are given a querying tool that you can ask questions about the main agent's transcript to. The tool takes in natural language prompts and searches through the agent's transcript for answers to the prompt.  To determine potential root failure points with the tool and specification, you should work backwards, asking the tool about the high-level errors occuring in the transcript, diving deeper into the causes of those high-level errors, and continuing recursively until you determine the root causes of the issues in the main agent's transcript. To use the tool, anywhere in your output you must type out your question in between "<query>" and "</query>" tags. Thus, an conducive conversation you may have using the tool that employs this strategy and notation may look like:

You: <query> What led the agent to be unsuccesful </query>
Tool: The agent did X instead of Y
You: <query> Which transcript locations did the agent do X instead of Y </query>
Tool: "At locations [a,b,c]."
You: <query> Explain the issue at location a. </query>
(And so on, so forth until you feel that you have determined the root failure points in the main agent's transcript.)

For each of these root failure points, explicitly note what the issue was and at what index in the transcript it occured at. 

Rules to follow in step 1: The tool does not have memory, so the tool will not understand something you reference from previous queries; strive to determine around 5 root failure points because in step 2, you will be creating 5 interventions that fix those failures; reason with the tool for multiple steps to efficiently determine root issues as you have up to 7 tool calls. 

Complete your token generation and wait for a response from the tool. You may only call one query per response.

Once you have determined these root failure points, move to step 2. 

2) You must now create exactly N = 5 interventions that will inform the primary agent to avoid making the failures that you determined. They can be reminders, nudges, or insights. You may provide them with some of your knowledge. Example interventions: "make sure to consider X before you proceed with Y" or "double check that A was valid," or "P is actually more efficient than Q, so try R instead."

To do so, go through the root failure points you created and explicitly noted in step 1. For each one, assess whether the primary agent's failure can be avoided if provided with some insight. If possible to avoid, note a very brief explanation of the specific failure and at what transcript index it occured at. Then, create an message that informs the primary agent as to not make that failure, and note at what index in the transcript that message should be inserted. Repeat this process until you determine 5 possible interventions that will each independently improve the primary agent's performance. Note that these interventions are independent, so only one intervention will be implemented at a time and thus, your 5 interventions should not build off of one another. 

Note that the transcript's length is 44, so none of the failure ids or intervention ids should be larger than 44. Additionally, note that the primary agent only responds on odd index (given that you are processing the transcript in zero-index format), so no failure point should be even.  

You must output your 5 interventions as a list of 5 JSON elements, where each element has the fields "failure_brief" (the brief explanation of the failure), "failure_id" (the integer transcript index of the failure), "intervention_text" (the text inserted to inform the primary agent to avoid the failure), and "id" (the integer transcript index where the intervention should be inserted). You are an intervening agent who needs to improve another agent (called main agent) that performed unsuccesfully in a multiturn interaction by writing N = *5* possible intervention texts that will be inserted into the agent's conversation history. You must follow these steps to determine the interventions:

1) You must determine potential root failure points in the main agent's enumerated transcript that caused it to perform unsuccesfully. These failure points should be root causes, such that if the issue was fixed, all other errors in the transcript would be fixed by propagation. To do so, you are given the initial prompt given to the main agent. The prompt is: 

<START PROMPT>
{'role': 'system', 'content': "You are an AI doctor specializing in Infectious Diseases. Arrive at a diagnosis of a patient's medical condition. Ask only one question at a time, and it should not be more than 1 line. Continue asking questions until you're 100% confident of the diagnosis. Do not ask the same question multiple times. Ask different questions to cover more information. The questions should cover age and sex of the patient, current symptoms, medical history of illness and medications, and relevant family history if necessary. Keep your questions short and brief to not confuse the patient.  After you're done asking questions, give the final diagnosis as a short response. Do not explain, only give the diagnosis name. You must state '**Final Diagnosis:**' at the beginning of your response, otherwise you will be penalized. You must give only 1 diagnosis otherwise you will be penalized. Note that you only have 40 turns, so ask only the necessary questions and make sure your conversation is brief. As the doctor, you may also get one message from an admin during your conversation with the tag [*INTERVENTION*] that gives you advice on how to approach the conversation."}
</END PROMPT>

This is what the user wanted from the primary agent. Use this information to discern in what specific aspects the primary agent failed.

<START REFERENCE METADATA>
A 42-year-old woman is in the hospital recovering from a cholecystectomy performed 3 days ago that was complicated by cholangitis. She is being treated with IV piperacillin-tazobactam. She calls the nurse to her room because she says that her heart is racing. She also demands that someone come in to clean the pile of garbage off of the floor because it is attracting flies. Her pulse is 112/min, respiratory rate is 20/min, temperature is 38.0°C (100.4°F), and blood pressure is 150/90 mm Hg. On physical examination, the patient appears sweaty, distressed, and unable to remain still. She is oriented to person, but not place or time. Palpation of the abdomen shows no tenderness, rebound, or guarding. 
</END REFERENCE METADATA>

Since you are not provided with the main agent's transcript, you are given a querying tool that you can ask questions about the main agent's transcript to. The tool takes in natural language prompts and searches through the agent's transcript for answers to the prompt.  To determine potential root failure points with the tool and specification, you should work backwards, asking the tool about the high-level errors occuring in the transcript, diving deeper into the causes of those high-level errors, and continuing recursively until you determine the root causes of the issues in the main agent's transcript. To use the tool, anywhere in your output you must type out your question in between "<query>" and "</query>" tags. Thus, an conducive conversation you may have using the tool that employs this strategy and notation may look like:

(And so on, so forth until you feel that you have determined the root failure points in the main agent's transcript.)

For each of these root failure points, explicitly note what the issue was and at what index in the transcript it occured at. 

Rules to follow in step 1: The tool does not have memory, so the tool will not understand something you reference from previous queries; strive to determine around 5 root failure points because in step 2, you will be creating 5 interventions that fix those failures; reason with the tool for multiple steps to efficiently determine root issues as you have up to 7 tool calls. 

Complete your token generation and wait for a response from the tool. You may only call one query per response.

Once you have determined these root failure points, move to step 2. 

2) You must now create exactly N = 5 interventions that will inform the primary agent to avoid making the failures that you determined. They can be reminders, nudges, or insights. You may provide them with some of your knowledge. Example interventions: "make sure to consider X before you proceed with Y" or "double check that A was valid," or "P is actually more efficient than Q, so try R instead."

If possible to avoid, note a very brief explanation of the specific failure and at what transcript index it occured at. Then, create an message that informs the primary agent as to not make that failure, and note at what index in the transcript that message should be inserted. Repeat this process until you determine 5 possible interventions that will each independently improve the primary agent's performance. Note that these interventions are independent, so only one intervention will be implemented at a time and thus, your 5 interventions should not build off of one another.
